metrics.narrative: Gerald articulated specific operational pain on the 8 Jul 2025 and 28 Apr 2026 calls but never quantified the business impact in dollar or cycle-time terms. Pain points identified: (1) fragmented vendor/contract management across three systems (SAI Global Compliance 360 for contracts, PeopleSoft for AP/vendor master, separate spreadsheet-based third-party risk workbook), (2) inability to generate cross-vendor risk reports (cannot answer 'show me all vendors with subcontractors' or 'all vendors with data access'), (3) wholly manual desktop audits for debarment, OFAC, DMV, SAR/SSI scores, and (4) over-customization of purchased software leading to brittleness. Gerald described the current state as 'mostly on paper' and 'not ideal' but effective due to 10 years of process refinement. He expressed a long-standing frustration: 'I've been promised a system for about 10 years' (8 Jul 2025 call). However, no savings target, headcount reduction goal, audit-finding remediation cost, or cycle-time improvement was stated. The pain is real and confirmed, but unquantified. To convert this to a business case, the deal team must co-develop a quantified ROI model with Gerald and the procurement leadership showing consolidation savings (eliminating up to 3 vendors per Gerald's goal) and efficiency gains from automation.

champion.narrative: Gerald Stiver (Manager, Vendor Compliance) is a developing coach, not yet a champion. He is the sole engaged contact, has attended both intro calls (8 Jul 2025 and 28 Apr 2026), understands the pain deeply (he owns third-party risk and feels the manual burden daily), and has shown genuine interest in Zycus capabilities. On both calls he asked detailed, probing questions about risk automation, AI contract analysis, OFAC checks, data hosting, out-of-box vs custom config, and pricing—demonstrating real evaluation intent, not passive listening. He explicitly stated consolidation as a strategic goal: 'I'm thinking how many programs this could replace. The play is to say we can eliminate 3 vendors if we use one' (8 Jul 2025). He also committed to share Zycus materials with the procurement team and facilitate a demo if they want one (28 Apr 2026). However, Gerald has NOT demonstrated advocacy or access to power. He has not introduced Zycus to the procurement leadership, the CIO, the CFO, or any executive sponsor in the 59 days since the last call. He explicitly stated he is outside his lane on budget and purchasing decisions ('that's not even really my conversation'). He has not championed Zycus internally in any visible way—no follow-up email, no request for a demo, no urgency. His engagement has gone silent since 7 May 2026 (50 days ago). Gerald is interested and aligned on the pain, but he is passive, not active. He is waiting for organizational readiness (1.5-2 years out per his own timeline) rather than pushing for it. To convert Gerald from coach to champion, Zycus must: (1) re-engage him with a high-value, low-effort next step (e.g., send the promised Showpad content and risk-assessment report he asked for on both calls but may not have received), (2) ask him directly to make one warm introduction to the procurement leadership or the CIO, and (3) co-develop a lightweight business case (consolidation savings, audit time saved, risk visibility improved) that Gerald can use to advocate internally when the transformation window opens. Gerald is cooling but recoverable.

competition.narrative: No named competitor has been mentioned by the buyer across two calls. The competitive landscape is incumbency and inertia, not a named vendor rival. The true 'competition' is: (1) Status quo / do nothing—Gerald has refined a manual, spreadsheet-based process over 10 years that 'works' and is 'pretty effective for a spreadsheet' (28 Apr 2026 call), reducing urgency to change. (2) SAI Global (now Intelex) Compliance 360—the incumbent GRC tool used for contract workflow and third-party risk. Gerald stated 'I hate it, to be perfectly honest' (8 Jul 2025) and 'it really doesn't provide a lot to us' (28 Apr 2026), but it is entrenched and the pain of replacing it (migrating 20,000 contracts) is a known barrier. Gerald noted 'there's PTSD in the company that says we're not moving contracts again' after a painful prior migration. (3) PeopleSoft (Oracle)—the ERP system that holds the vendor master and AP workflows, described as 'pretty entrenched, used for a long time' (28 Apr 2026). Gerald indicated Peoplesoft would likely stay in place even if Zycus were adopted, meaning Zycus would need to integrate with it, not replace it. (4) The 1.5-2 year core IT transformation (mainframe COBOL to cloud)—this is the organizationally-consuming priority that is blocking all other software evaluation and budget allocation. It is the REAL competitor in the sense that it owns all executive attention, IT resources, and budget, leaving no room for a procurement-software decision until circa 2028. (5) Budget scarcity and austerity—Gerald stated twice (8 Jul 2025 and 28 Apr 2026) that the company is on an austerity plan and price-conscious, with an implied ceiling under $200k/year. If Zycus pricing exceeds that or if no budget is allocated post-transformation, the deal dies to 'no budget' rather than to a named competitor. The wedge: Zycus must position as a POST-transformation quick win—a low-touch, out-of-box, modular add that consolidates 3 vendors, automates Gerald's manual pain, delivers fast ROI, and integrates with (not replaces) PeopleSoft. Timing the re-engagement for early 2028 (when transformation completes) is critical. If Zycus waits passively, the status quo wins by default.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is confirmed, detailed, and owned by Gerald Stiver (Manager, Vendor Compliance). Gerald articulated the same pain points consistently across two calls nearly a year apart (8 Jul 2025 and 28 Apr 2026), indicating persistent, unresolved operational friction. Confirmed pain points: (1) Fragmented vendor and contract management—three disconnected systems (SAI Global Compliance 360 for contract workflow, PeopleSoft for AP and vendor master, manual Excel-based third-party risk workbook)—leading to operational inefficiency and inability to get a unified view. (2) Manual, time-intensive desktop audits for every vendor: searching Pennsylvania and federal debarment lists, OFAC terrorist watchlist, DMV reports, SAR and SSI credit/risk scores—all done by hand, repeatedly, with no automation. (3) Inability to run cross-vendor risk queries and reports—Gerald cannot answer basic questions like 'show me all vendors with subcontractors' or 'all vendors with access to sensitive data' because the data lives in spreadsheets and siloed systems. (4) Over-customization of purchased software leading to brittleness and breakage—Gerald stated 'my company has a penchant for buying out-of-box software and completely over-customizing it until it breaks,' creating technical debt and resistance to new tools. (5) Long-standing frustration and broken promises—'I've been promised a better system for about 10 years' (8 Jul 2025 call). (6) Anticipated resource constraints—Gerald noted the company is 'trying to have less people, so better automation is necessary at some point.' Pain is real, operational, and day-to-day for Gerald's team. However, the pain has NOT been escalated to or acknowledged by executive leadership (no CIO, CFO, or VP involvement), and the company's current priority is the core mainframe-to-cloud transformation, which has consumed IT resources and budget for 3 years and will continue for another 1.5-2 years per Gerald. So while the pain is confirmed and chronic, it is NOT urgent or top-of-mind for the executive layer, and it is NOT driving a near-term buying decision.

decision_criteria.narrative: Gerald articulated a clear set of functional requirements across both calls, but these are HIS criteria as the risk/compliance user, not a formalized, cross-functional buying committee's decision criteria. Requirements stated: (1) Automated vendor risk profiling with integration to external data sources (debarment lists, OFAC, DMV, SAR/SSI scores, Dun & Bradstreet, LexisNexis); (2) dynamic inherent risk assessment tool that can report comprehensive cross-vendor risk data (e.g., all vendors with subcontractors, all with data access); (3) ability to replace/consolidate up to three existing systems (SAI Global, manual risk workbook, potentially parts of PeopleSoft workflow); (4) out-of-the-box solution with minimal customization (Gerald noted 'my company has a penchant for buying out-of-box software and completely over-customizing it until it breaks'); (5) cloud-based SaaS with flexible deployment and domestic data hosting due to state agency data sovereignty concerns (Gerald flagged international storage as 'could be a showstopper'); (6) AI-powered contract analysis (auto-extract key terms, flag non-standard clauses, suggest negotiation points); (7) modular pricing to fit budget constraints (under $200k/year implied ceiling). These are well-defined use-case needs but have NOT been validated by procurement leadership, IT, legal, or an executive sponsor. No RFP, BRD, or formal evaluation rubric has been mentioned. The buyer has not run a structured vendor comparison or scoring process. Gerald's interest is real, but criteria remain at the individual-contributor level, not the enterprise buying-committee level. To confirm decision criteria, Zycus must engage the broader procurement team and economic buyer.

items.risk: Medium. VP IT likely influences procurement software decisions, especially cloud/SaaS and integration with PeopleSoft. Gerald noted IT resources are constrained by the transformation, so IT stakeholder buy-in is critical. Has never been introduced or engaged.
items.title: Vice President, Information Technology

items.detail: Buyer has explicitly stated a 1.5-2 year purchasing timeline (circa Q4 2027 to Q1 2028) due to ongoing 3-year mainframe-to-cloud transformation that is consuming all IT resources and budget. Close date 14 Oct 2026 (110 days out) is a placeholder with no credible path to close. Deal is in long-nurture mode, not active evaluation.
items.status: Active blocker. Buyer will not evaluate or purchase procurement software until core IT transformation completes.

items.detail: Buyer stated company is on austerity plan (8 Jul 2025) and 'really price conscious' with an implied ceiling under $200k/year (28 Apr 2026). No budget has been allocated or discussed. Gerald has no budget authority. CFO not engaged. Even if timeline aligns in 2028, budget approval is uncertain.
items.status: Active blocker. No budget allocated, no EB engaged to allocate it, and price sensitivity flagged.

items.detail: No executive sponsor, Economic Buyer, or procurement leadership engaged. Deal is single-threaded through Gerald Stiver (Manager, Vendor Compliance), who has no budget authority, no decision-making power, and has not introduced Zycus to the buying committee in 59 days since the last call. Without executive alignment, deal has no path to budget approval or decision.
items.status: Critical gap. No executive engagement, no multi-threading, no path to decision.

items.detail: Buyer has organizational trauma from a prior contract-system migration (moved 20,000 contracts into SAI Global Compliance 360 in a painful effort years ago). Gerald stated 'there's PTSD in the company that says we're not moving 20,000 contracts again,' creating resistance to replacing the incumbent contract system even though Gerald dislikes it. This is a significant adoption and change-management barrier.
items.status: Active barrier. Migration fear is blocking consideration of contract-system replacement (iContract module).

items.detail: Buyer is a Pennsylvania state agency with data-sovereignty concerns. Gerald flagged international data storage (India) as a potential showstopper and stated he would need to consult IT and InfoSec (28 Apr 2026). Grace committed to confirm domestic-only hosting option and send security documentation, but no confirmation was provided in the 59 days since. InfoSec review and data-residency approval are unresolved and may become a deal-killer if not addressed before re-engagement.
items.status: Unresolved. Potential showstopper if Zycus cannot offer domestic-only hosting or if InfoSec rejects international data storage.

items.detail: Buyer uses Oracle PeopleSoft as the ERP for vendor master and AP workflows, described as 'pretty entrenched, used for a long time' (28 Apr 2026). Zycus must integrate with PeopleSoft bidirectionally (vendor data sync) for the deal to work; PeopleSoft will not be replaced. Grace stated Zycus has open API and Oracle integration experience, but no technical validation, integration scoping, or IT review has occurred. Integration feasibility and effort are unconfirmed.
items.status: Unvalidated. Integration requirement confirmed by buyer; technical feasibility and scoping not yet validated by IT stakeholders.

items.detail: Gerald noted organizational flux: 'We traditionally have been in procurement, now we're out of procurement, but we're moving back into procurement' (28 Apr 2026), indicating unclear reporting lines and shifting org structure. This creates uncertainty about who owns the procurement software decision (is it procurement, IT, shared services, finance?) and may delay or complicate the buying process even when budget and timeline align.
items.status: Low-grade risk. Org-structure flux may complicate stakeholder mapping and decision process when deal reactivates.

champion_strength.source: SF OpportunityContactRole (Evaluator); Avoma calls 8 Jul 2025 and 28 Apr 2026; no activity since 7 May 2026 (50 days)
champion_strength.summary: Gerald Stiver is a developing coach, not yet a champion. He understands the pain deeply (owns third-party risk, feels the manual burden daily), is aligned on Zycus value (risk automation, AI contract analysis, consolidation goal), and has engaged twice (8 Jul 2025 and 28 Apr 2026) with detailed, probing questions. However, he has shown no advocacy—no introduction to procurement leadership, no demo request, no follow-up—in the 59 days since the last call. He explicitly stated he has no budget authority ('that's not my conversation') and is waiting for organizational readiness 1.5-2 years out rather than pushing for it internally. Gerald is interested but passive. He is cooling due to silence but recoverable if re-engaged with high-value deliverables and equipped with a business case to advocate when the window opens.

items.action: Re-engage Gerald Stiver with the overdue deliverables he requested and ask for one warm intro to the procurement team lead or the deal owner's manager.
items.trigger: Gerald went silent 50 days ago after requesting Showpad content, risk report, and security docs on 28 Apr 2026 call. Grace committed to send them by early May per Next Step logged 7 May, but no evidence they were delivered. Gerald also committed to share materials with procurement team and facilitate demo, but no such introduction materialized.
items.expected_effect: Fulfilling overdue commitments re-establishes credibility, gives Gerald the tools he asked for to advocate internally, and tests whether he is still engaged. The warm-intro ask (one name, one email address—procurement lead or the deal owner's manager) is a low-friction litmus test of his willingness to champion. If he declines or ignores, the deal is dormant and should be moved to long-nurture (2028 re-engagement). If he makes the intro, the deal multi-threads and a path to the Economic Buyer opens.

items.action: Co-develop a one-page business case quantifying consolidation savings and manual-audit time saved, and send it to Gerald for internal advocacy.
items.trigger: Gerald articulated clear operational pain (manual desktop audits, fragmented 3-system landscape, no cross-vendor risk reporting, 10-year wait for a better system) on both calls but never quantified the cost or ROI. He stated the strategic goal is to 'eliminate 3 vendors if we use one' (8 Jul 2025). No business case or ROI model has been developed.
items.expected_effect: A lightweight, one-page ROI model (hours saved per vendor audit, cost of running 3 systems vs 1, risk-visibility improvement, compliance/audit-finding reduction) equips Gerald to advocate internally when the IT transformation completes and procurement software rises on the priority list (circa Q4 2027 to Q1 2028). Without a defensible business case, Gerald has no ammunition to push for Zycus when the window opens, and the status quo wins by default. The business case should cite state-agency peer examples (e.g., 'Agency X automated vendor risk assessments and cut audit cycle time by 60%, consolidating 3 tools into Zycus') to make it credible and relevant.

items.action: Confirm domestic-only hosting option and send complete InfoSec package to Gerald for IT and InfoSec pre-clearance before re-engagement window opens.
items.trigger: Gerald flagged international data storage (India) as a potential showstopper for Pennsylvania state agency on 28 Apr 2026 call. Grace committed to confirm domestic-only hosting option and send security documentation, but no confirmation was provided in 59 days since.
items.expected_effect: De-risks a known deal-killer. If Zycus cannot offer domestic-only hosting or if InfoSec review is a multi-month process, better to surface and resolve it now (during long-nurture phase) than at the moment the buying window opens in 2028. Sending the InfoSec package (SOC 2, data-residency options, SaaS architecture, compliance certs) to Gerald now allows him to route it to IT/InfoSec for informal pre-clearance, so when the deal reactivates in 2028, InfoSec approval is a known quantity, not a surprise blocker. If InfoSec rejects international storage and Zycus has no domestic option, this deal is dead and should be disqualified now rather than nursed for 18 months.

items.action: Set a firm calendar trigger for Q4 2027 re-engagement and build a structured quarterly nurture plan with high-value content until then.
items.trigger: Gerald stated on 28 Apr 2026 call that the organization is 1.5-2 years away from procurement software appetite and budget due to ongoing mainframe-to-cloud transformation. Timeline: re-engage circa Oct 2027 to Jan 2028 when transformation is wrapping and procurement software rises on priority list.
items.expected_effect: Prevents this deal from falling into a black hole. The buyer has given us a firm timeline; ignoring it (forcing a near-term close) is futile, but going silent for 18 months is equally fatal (Gerald will forget Zycus, the status quo will calcify, or a competitor will own the moment). The win strategy is structured low-touch nurture: quarterly value content (state-agency procurement case studies, risk-automation ROI calculators, new Merlin AI capabilities that automate Gerald's exact pain, Horizon event invite if timing aligns) plus a firm Q4 2027 calendar trigger. At that moment, re-engage with: 'Your transformation is wrapping; let's make Zycus the FIRST post-transformation optimization—a fast (3-6 months), modular SaaS add that integrates with your new cloud stack, automates the manual work your team has done for 10 years, and consolidates 3 legacy vendors.' If Zycus owns that moment with a warm champion (Gerald) and a business case in hand, the deal reopens. If Zycus is absent or mistimes it, the status quo wins.

items.action: Request a 15-minute stakeholder-mapping call with Gerald to identify procurement leadership and the deal owner's manager by name and role before the 2028 window opens.
items.trigger: Deal is single-threaded through Gerald (Manager, Vendor Compliance, no budget authority) with zero visibility into the buying committee. CFO Kyle Lefever, CIO Michael Hendler, VP Shared Services Jessica Nieves, and VP IT Melissa Shoemaker are on the account but not engaged. Gerald mentioned 'the procurement team' and 'the deal owner's manager' but never named them.
items.expected_effect: Maps the real buying committee (who owns procurement software decisions: VP Procurement? CIO? CFO?) and the path to the Economic Buyer before the 2028 re-engagement window opens. The goal is not to force an introduction now (the buyer is not ready), but to know WHO to target when the window opens. Frame the ask as low-friction: 'Gerald, when your team is ready to evaluate procurement software in 2028, who would typically own that decision—is it procurement, IT, the CFO? And who would you report the business case to—the deal owner's manager or a VP?' This gives Zycus the stakeholder map to plan the 2028 re-engagement (e.g., target the CIO with a technical integration story, the CFO with the consolidation ROI, the VP Procurement with the operational pain Gerald described). Without this map, Zycus re-engages blind in 2028 and loses to a competitor who already threaded the committee.

items: Positive: Gerald asked detailed, use-case-driven questions on both calls (8 Jul 2025 and 28 Apr 2026)—risk automation, AI contract analysis, OFAC checks, data hosting, out-of-box config, pricing, implementation timeline—indicating real evaluation intent and genuine interest, not passive listening.

Positive: Gerald articulated a clear strategic goal—consolidate 3 vendors into one system (8 Jul 2025 and 28 Apr 2026)—and stated 'that's the play at some point in my company,' showing organizational alignment on the consolidation business case.
Positive: Gerald committed on 28 Apr 2026 to share Zycus materials with the procurement team and facilitate a demo if they want more information, indicating willingness to advocate internally.

Negative: 50 days of silence since last activity (7 May 2026). No reply from Gerald, no demo scheduled, no procurement team introduced, no follow-up email or engagement. The deal has gone cold.

Negative: Grace committed on both calls to send high-value deliverables (Showpad content, risk-assessment report, security docs, confirmation of domestic hosting and data-provider subscriptions), but there is no evidence these were sent or that Gerald received them. Unfulfilled commitments on both sides signal low prioritization.

Negative: Deal is single-threaded through Gerald (Manager, Vendor Compliance), who has no budget authority and has not multi-threaded to procurement leadership, CIO, CFO, or any executive sponsor in 59 days since the last call. Single-threading with a non-buyer contact is a losing position.

Negative: No Economic Buyer, no Decision Maker, no executive sponsor, no procurement leadership engaged. No buying committee, no RFP, no formal evaluation process activated. Deal is pre-qualification, not active evaluation.

Negative: Close date 14 Oct 2026 (110 days out) is a placeholder with no credible path to close. Forecast category is Pipeline, but even that is indefensible given the buyer's stated 1.5-2 year timeline. This should be Omitted (long-nurture, 2028 opportunity).
confidence_signals.summary: This deal has two contradictory signals: (1) Gerald's detailed, probing questions and explicit consolidation goal across two calls signal real interest and a genuine use case, BUT (2) his stated 1.5-2 year timeline, the all-consuming IT transformation, 50 days of silence, and zero multi-threading or advocacy signal that this is a long-nurture, low-probability near-term opportunity. The buyer is interested but not ready, and the deal is cooling due to lack of follow-through on both sides.

north_star_verdict.math: Created 29 Apr 2026, last buyer activity 7 May 2026 (50 days ago vs today 26 Jun). Close date 14 Oct 2026 is 110 days out. Intro call was 28 Apr 2026 (59 days ago), discovery call referenced in Next Step never occurred. Stage is Qualified but no forward movement since early May. The buyer explicitly stated on 28 Apr 2026 that they are 1.5-2 years away from procurement software purchasing decisions due to ongoing mainframe-to-cloud transformation and budget constraints. Next Step dated to early May (send Showpad content and schedule discovery call in 3 weeks from 7 May, which would have been ~28 May) was never completed. No buyer engagement, no reply, and no scheduled call materialized. This is a live conversation that went silent, not a cold deal from the start, but momentum has clearly stalled.
evidence: Intro call 8 Jul 2025 (nearly 1 year ago): Gerald expressed interest but stated company on austerity plan, not ready to buy for a year or two (source: Avoma transcript 8 Jul 2025).

Second intro call 28 Apr 2026 (59 days ago): Gerald confirmed ongoing 3-year mainframe-to-COBOL-to-cloud transformation, stated appetite and budget will not materialize for another 1.5-2 years (source: Avoma transcript 28 Apr 2026).

Grace committed on 28 Apr call to send Showpad shared space with demo videos and resources; Next Step on 7 May says 'Sending Gerald Showpad content that he shares with the larger procurement team - Getting in touch with the team for a Discovery call with them in 3 weeks' (source: SF Next_Step__c).
No evidence that Showpad content was sent, no evidence of discovery call scheduled or completed, no reply from Gerald since 7 May, no new Tasks or Events logged since early May.
LastActivityDate 7 May 2026 is 50 days ago; no buyer-facing deliverable or engagement since then.
Close date 14 Oct 2026 (110 days out) is a placeholder with no credible path to close given the 1.5-2 year timeline stated by the buyer and the current silence.
north_star_verdict.headline: Deal remains stalled 50 days with zero buyer contact since early May. Buyer explicitly positioned purchasing 1.5-2 years out due to core IT transformation and austerity. Discovery call never materialized; close date placeholder lacks credible path.

best_practice_check.summary: This deal has gone silent for 50 days after an engaged intro call, and the buyer has explicitly stated a 1.5-2 year purchasing timeline, making it a long-nurture, low-probability near-term opportunity. Three strategic levers will determine whether Zycus wins when the window reopens in 2028: (1) staying warm with Gerald through low-touch, high-value nurture so he remains an internal advocate when budget and appetite materialize, (2) building a lightweight, defensible business case (consolidation savings, audit time saved, risk visibility) that Gerald can use to champion Zycus internally at the right moment, and (3) timing the re-engagement for Q4 2027 or Q1 2028 when the core IT transformation wraps and procurement software rises on the priority list. If Zycus goes silent or re-engages prematurely, the status quo wins by default.

competitive_position.summary: No named external competitor. The competitive landscape is incumbency and inertia. SAI Global (Intelex) Compliance 360 is the entrenched incumbent GRC tool, disliked but embedded, with a known migration pain (20,000 contracts, organizational PTSD from prior move). PeopleSoft is the entrenched ERP for vendor master and AP, not replacing. The true competition is the status quo (manual Excel-based process that 'works' per Gerald), the 1.5-2 year core IT transformation that is consuming all resources and budget, and budget scarcity (austerity plan, price ceiling under $200k/year). The deal will be won or lost on timing (re-engaging post-transformation in early 2028) and positioning Zycus as a fast, low-touch, consolidation play that integrates with PeopleSoft and delivers ROI by eliminating 3 vendors. If Zycus does not re-engage at the right moment, the status quo wins by default.

competitors.name: Status Quo / Do Nothing
competitors.quote: Our procurement workbook is an Excel spreadsheet we've developed with scoring and due diligence list generation. It's actually pretty effective for a spreadsheet. We've gotten good at it. I've had the same analyst for 10 years.
competitors.how_we_win: Quantify the hidden cost and risk of the manual status quo—audit time, error rate, inability to scale, compliance exposure from incomplete risk data. Co-build a lightweight ROI model with Gerald showing consolidation savings (eliminating up to 3 vendors per his goal) and efficiency gains (hours saved per vendor onboarding/audit). Position Zycus as the post-transformation quick win that automates his daily pain with minimal IT lift.

competitors.name: SAI Global (Intelex) Compliance 360
competitors.quote: We use SAI Global Compliance 360. I hate it, to be perfectly honest. It really doesn't provide a lot to us. The company uses it more as a contract workflow and contract repository. Our program is mostly on paper.
competitors.source: Avoma calls 8 Jul 2025 and 28 Apr 2026, Gerald Stiver
competitors.how_we_win: Gerald dislikes the incumbent and it provides little value, but the pain of migrating 20,000 contracts is the barrier ('there's PTSD in the company—we're not moving contracts again'). Neutralize the migration fear with a phased approach: start with iRisk (vendor risk automation, Gerald's primary pain) and iSupplier (vendor portal) FIRST, which do not require contract migration and can run alongside Compliance 360. Demonstrate fast time-to-value (3-6 months per Grace's statement) and prove low-touch integration with PeopleSoft. Once Gerald's team sees ROI and the transformation completes, phase in iContract to replace Compliance 360 with a structured, Zycus-led migration plan that minimizes internal effort.

competitors.name: Oracle PeopleSoft (ERP - Vendor Master and AP)
competitors.quote: We keep vendors in PeopleSoft, pretty entrenched, used for a long time.
competitors.how_we_win: Do not position Zycus as a PeopleSoft replacement—position as a value-add layer that integrates with it. Grace stated on 28 Apr 'we can plug in with what you already have; you could leave PeopleSoft to manage vendors if that's what's working well.' Emphasize Zycus open API and proven Oracle PeopleSoft integration so vendor master data flows bidirectionally. This removes a replacement-fear objection and makes Zycus an easier, lower-risk add.

competitors.name: Core IT Transformation (Mainframe to Cloud) - Organizational Priority Blocker
competitors.quote: We're in a 3-year project moving off mainframe COBOL to cloud. My organization is still a year or two out. Once that occurs we'll understand what tools we can interact with. Probably 1.5 to 2 years before they start thinking about peripheral softwares. There will be a better appetite and budget in about 2 years.
competitors.how_we_win: This is the real gatekeeper. All executive attention, IT resources, and budget are locked in the transformation through circa Q4 2027 / Q1 2028. Trying to force a decision now is futile. The win strategy is TIMING: stay warm with Gerald via low-touch nurture (quarterly value content: risk-automation ROI calculators, state-agency case studies, new AI capabilities in iRisk/Merlin), and set a firm re-engagement trigger for Q4 2027 or Q1 2028 when transformation is wrapping. At that moment, position Zycus as the FIRST post-transformation optimization—a fast, modular, SaaS add that integrates with the new cloud stack, delivers ROI in 3-6 months, and consolidates 3 legacy vendors. If Zycus re-engages at the right moment with a warm champion (Gerald) and a business case in hand, the deal reopens. If Zycus goes silent or re-engages too early, the status quo wins.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI positioning was strong and resonated with Gerald across both calls. Gerald's agency has an explicit goal to 'integrate some AI and streamline processes' (28 Apr 2026), and he asked detailed questions about AI contract analysis (auto-extract terms, flag non-standard clauses, suggest negotiation points), which Grace confirmed Zycus delivers via generative AI and the Merlin platform. Gerald also expressed interest in AI-driven risk automation to replace manual desktop audits. However, the AI story was presented as a feature set, not as a strategic wedge or competitive differentiator tied to a compelling event. The Merlin agentic AI platform (autonomous agents for risk, negotiation, contracting) was mentioned but not deeply explored or anchored to Gerald's specific pain (e.g., 'Merlin can autonomously run your 20-question inherent risk assessment and flag high-risk vendors in real time, eliminating your manual spreadsheet process'). To strengthen AI positioning, Zycus should: (1) tie Merlin AI directly to Gerald's manual pain points with a use-case demo or video showing his exact workflow automated, and (2) position AI as the post-transformation quick win—'Your new cloud stack is modern; add Merlin as the AI layer that makes procurement intelligent and automates the manual work your team has done for 10 years.' AI resonated but was under-leveraged as a wedge.

items.criterion: Automated vendor risk profiling with external data integrations (debarment, OFAC, DMV, SAR/SSI scores, DNB, LexisNexis)

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Zycus capabilities align well with Gerald's stated functional requirements (risk automation, AI contract analysis, modular deployment, out-of-box config, cloud SaaS), but three gaps create fit risk: (1) data sovereignty—international data storage (India) is a potential showstopper for a Pennsylvania state agency; Grace said domestic-only hosting is possible but needs confirmation. (2) Pricing—Gerald stated an implied ceiling under $200k/year and flagged the company as 'really price conscious'; Zycus pricing was not disclosed but Grace indicated 'couple hundred thousand probably wouldn't be where we fall' and 'we're flexible,' leaving uncertainty. (3) Timing and organizational readiness—Gerald's 1.5-2 year timeline and the consuming IT transformation mean the buyer is NOT ready to evaluate or buy, regardless of fit. The functional fit is strong; the commercial and timing fit is uncertain and must be de-risked before re-engagement.

Grace committed to confirm domestic-only hosting option and send security documentation, but no confirmation was provided in the 59 days since. InfoSec review and data-residency approval are unresolved and may become a deal-killer if not addressed before re-engagement.

value.detail: Buyer uses Oracle PeopleSoft as the ERP for vendor master and AP workflows, described as 'pretty entrenched, used for a long time' (28 Apr 2026). Zycus must integrate with PeopleSoft bidirectionally (vendor data sync) for the deal to work; PeopleSoft will not be replaced. Grace stated Zycus has open API and Oracle integration experience, but no technical validation, integration scoping, or IT review has occurred. Integration feasibility and effort are unconfirmed.
value.source: Avoma call 28 Apr 2026, Gerald: 'We keep vendors in PeopleSoft, pretty entrenched.' Grace: 'We can plug in with what you have, we have open API and I'm familiar with Oracle.'
value.status: Unvalidated. Integration requirement confirmed by buyer; technical feasibility and scoping not yet validated by IT stakeholders.

value.detail: Gerald noted organizational flux: 'We traditionally have been in procurement, now we're out of procurement, but we're moving back into procurement' (28 Apr 2026), indicating unclear reporting lines and shifting org structure. This creates uncertainty about who owns the procurement software decision (is it procurement, IT, shared services, finance?) and may delay or complicate the buying process even when budget and timeline align.

value.flag: Deal is single-threaded through Gerald Stiver (Manager, Vendor Compliance), who has no budget authority and has not introduced Zycus to procurement leadership, CIO, CFO, or any executive sponsor in 59 days since the last call. Multi-threading is the highest-leverage near-term move: re-engage Gerald with the overdue deliverables (Showpad content, risk report, security docs) and ask for one warm introduction to the procurement team lead or the CIO. A single-threaded deal with a non-buyer contact is unwinnable.

value.flag: Champion development is stalled. Gerald is interested and aligned on the pain but has shown no advocacy (no follow-up, no demo request, no introduction, no reply in 50 days). To convert him from passive coach to active champion, co-develop a lightweight, one-page business case that quantifies the pain he described (manual audit hours per vendor, cost of running 3 systems vs 1, risk-visibility gaps, consolidation ROI) and equip him to advocate internally when the transformation completes. Without an active champion pushing when the window opens, this deal loses to inertia.

value.flag: Timing and nurture strategy must be explicit. The buyer has stated a 1.5-2 year timeline (re-engage circa Q4 2027 or Q1 2028). Forcing a near-term decision is futile; passive silence is equally fatal. The win strategy is structured low-touch nurture: quarterly value content (state-agency procurement case studies, risk-automation ROI calculators, new Merlin AI capabilities in iRisk that automate Gerald's exact pain), plus a firm calendar trigger for Q4 2027 re-engagement. At that moment, position Zycus as the FIRST post-transformation optimization: a fast (3-6 months), modular SaaS add that integrates with the new cloud stack and consolidates 3 legacy vendors. If Zycus owns that moment with a warm champion and a business case in hand, the deal reopens. If Zycus is absent or mistimes it, the status quo wins.

scope

gaps: No buyer-side attendees beyond Gerald Stiver on either call; no procurement team, CIO, CFO, or executive sponsor ever engaged despite Gerald's stated intent to share materials with procurement team (28 Apr 2026).

No evidence that Grace's committed deliverables (Showpad content, risk-assessment report, security docs, domestic-hosting confirmation, data-provider subscription confirmation) were sent after 28 Apr or 8 Jul 2025 calls—no follow-up emails, no Tasks logged, no reply from Gerald confirming receipt.

No field history for stage movement, amount changes, or close-date slips (opportunity created 29 Apr 2026, only one month of history exists; prior 8 Jul 2025 call predates opp creation so no Salesforce record of it).
No Tasks or Events logged since early May 2026; LastActivityDate 7 May is a system-generated field, not a logged buyer engagement.
No AIS (AI Score) populated in Salesforce despite buyer stating an organizational goal to integrate AI (28 Apr 2026 call).

No custom MEDDPICC fields (Economic Buyer Identified, Decision Maker Identified, Champion Identified, Pain Identified, Metrics Identified) populated in Salesforce; all are blank or do not exist in this org.

Competitor field unknown in Salesforce and no competitor named by buyer on either call; competitive landscape is inferred from incumbent tools (SAI Global, PeopleSoft) and status quo, not from a named rival.
